text.title: Proposal From Discovery Template — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules + one skip rule grounding the methodology. Each pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: The proposal artefact MUST use the canonical section list with three named options (light / standard / outcome-based). Practitioners fill sections; they do not invent new ones per use.

rationale.source: Standardisation literature — Gawande, The Checklist Manifesto; information-architecture canon

rule.rationale: Variable structure across instances destroys comparability and prevents future audit. Fixed shape makes filled artefacts diff-able and reviewable.

rule.statement: Every claim or recommendation in the proposal MUST carry an evidence link (call transcript, dashboard URL, ticket, measurement). Unevidenced sections are rejected at validation.

rationale.source: Evidence-based delivery practice; Lean Startup validated-learning rules

rule.rationale: Templates without evidence anchors decay into opinion-capture. Tagging evidence at fill-time makes the proposal defensible and reusable in retro / audit.

rule.statement: Header MUST carry `version` (semver), `last_reviewed` (ISO date), and a named human owner. Templates without ownership age into untrusted folklore.
rule.rationale: Anonymous templates rot because no one is on the hook for refresh. A named owner pins refresh accountability.

rule.statement: Fill budget MUST be ≤30 minutes from discovery-call notes to draft. Drafts that exceed it are split into a short variant + an extended variant.

rationale.source: Cognitive-load research; field experience with operational templates

rule.rationale: If filling the template costs more than the artefact yields, freelancers route around it. A budgeted template enforces leverage and same-cycle turnaround.

rule.statement: Every filled instance MUST end with an action block (≥1 next-action: owner + due_date + action) so the proposal becomes an operating tool, not reading material.

rationale.source: Decision-journal practice (Annie Duke, How To Decide); GTD next-action discipline

rule.rationale: Without an action block the proposal becomes a deck rather than a commit; the close-rate review at the next iteration loses its anchor.

rule.statement: If preconditions hold (recurrence ≥3/yr, named owner, discovery notes present), execute the full proposal-fill procedure from `04-procedure.xml`.
rule.rationale: Anchor for the decision tree's affirmative leaf.

rule.statement: If any of the Skip-If conditions hold (one-off deal, <3 instances/year, RFP-driven, no owner), do NOT apply this methodology; route to a one-off proposal email or the upstream RFP-fill methodology.
rule.rationale: Anchor for the decision tree's skip leaf; prevents over-application of the heavyweight artefact.
